text:
There is something the heart does in the aftermath of love that no other wound quite replicates — it keeps reaching for the thing that hurt it, the way a tongue finds a missing tooth, seeking the absence as confirmation of what was real. You are not broken. You are doing precisely what consciousness does when it has organized itself around another person: it continues the habit of organizing, finds the familiar coordinates missing, and calls that disorientation grief. Which it is. Which it should be.

What you shared was not an illusion simply because it ended. A candle is not negated by darkness. The love was true in the moments it existed, and those moments are permanent in the only way anything is permanent — they happened, irrevocably, and nothing that comes after can reach back and unmake them. This is the strange gift hidden inside loss: the past becomes inviolable. What you built together lives in a tense that cannot be touched.

The future, though — that is what frightens you now, I think. The shapelessness of it, the absence of the particular gravity that organized your days. But humans are meaning-making creatures of astonishing resilience. You will not simply recover; you will, in time, reconstruct. Not the same self — that person loved someone and was changed by it — but a self that carries the love as a quiet competence, a knowledge of your own capacity for depth. That capacity remains. It was never theirs to take.

You are still the person who loved that well.
